person.userCertifiedLifeSummary:
«b»Biography«/b»
Ivo de Harcourt was born about 1100 in Shenton, Leicestershire, England.

He died after 1166. 

Ivo was a major follower of Robert (II), Earl of Leicester. He is found frequently in the Leicester household, and accompanied his lord to Normandy in 1154.

"Willielmus de Harvecurt" donated "all of the land in Stanton" ("totam terram Stantunie") to Garendon abbey (Leicestershire), with the consent of "Ivo my brother and Agnes my mother" ("Yvonis fratris mei et Agnetis matris mee"), by charter dated 1148.

Pope Anastasius IV (1153/54) confirmed donations to Garendon abbey (Leicestershire), including the donation of "tertiam partem totius terre Stantune" made by "Willielmi de Haurucurth" with the consent of "Yvonis fratris sui et Agnetis matris ipsorum", by undated charter.

sourceList.title: Ivo de Harcourt, "Find A Grave Index"
Sir Egerton Brydges - Collins's peerage of England; genealogical, biographical, and historical, Volume 4 by Arthur Collins,

nameMeanings.name: d'Harcourt
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (of Norman origin) and French: habitational name from any of the places in Eure and Calvados named Harcourt, from Old French cour(t) ‘court, farmyard’ (see Court ) with an obscure first element. This surname is very rare in France.

English: habitational name from either of two places in Shropshire named Harcourt. The one near Cleobury Mortimer gets the name from Old English heafocere ‘hawker, falconer’ + cot ‘hut, cottage’; the one near Wem has as its first element Old English hearpere (see Harper ).

History: The surname of French origin (see 1 above) is listed along with the equally rare or even extinct variants or altered forms Haraucourt, Herancourt, and Héraucourt in the (US) National Huguenot Society's register of qualified Huguenot ancestors.

variants.name: Court
meanings: English: topographic or occupational name from Middle English curt, court ‘large house’ (a word of the same ultimately Latin origin as 2 below), denoting someone who lived or worked in a manor house or castle, a courtly retainer.
French: topographic name from Old French co(u)rt ‘court, farmyard’ (from Latin cohors, cors, genitive cohortis, cortis ‘court, farmyard, enclosure’).

English and French: nickname for a physically small man, or ironically for a large man, from the adjective Middle English curt, Old French co(u)rt ‘short, small’ (from Latin curtus ‘curtailed, truncated, cut short, broken off’). Compare French Lecourt .

nameMeanings.name: Ivo
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Form of   Yves   used in Germany and occasionally in the English-speaking world. It represents the nominative case of the Latinized form of the name.

en.lifeSummary: When Ivo d'Harcourt was born about 1100, in Shenton, Leicestershire, England, United Kingdom, his father, Robert d'Harcourt, was 62 and his mother, Agnes, was 27. He had at least 2 sons and 1 daughter with Rohese Peveral. He died after 1166, in Bourn, Cambridgeshire, England, United Kingdom.

ko.lifeSummary: Ivo d'Harcourt(이)가 대략 1100년 영국 잉글랜드 레이스스터실르 셰턴에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Robert d'Harcourt(은)는 62세였고, 그의 어머니 Agnes(은)는 27세였다. 그는 Rohese Peveral(와)과 적어도 두 명의 아들과 한 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 1166년 이후 영국 잉글랜드 캄브리지실르 부른에서 사망했다.
